clean_story:
New ideas. Old brain tricks.
The term metaphor comes from Greek metaphorá—“to carry over,” like using a bridge to move meaning from one idea to another.

Your brain isn’t built for abstract thoughts. “Time is money” works because it hijacks your wallet’s wiring: you feel urgency like losing cash, not just clock hands moving. fMRI scans show metaphors light up sensory areas—hearing “rough day” activates the same cortex as touching sandpaper. Not a figure of speech: a neural shortcut.

Example: When scientists called viruses “invasive armies,” public health messages stuck 40% better. Why? “Army” triggers ancient threat-detection circuits, bypassing slow logic. Same trick turns “climate data” into “Earth’s fever”—suddenly, graphs feel like a sick child.

Metaphors don’t just explain; they rewire. A 2018 study found students taught “electrons are crowded commuters” solved physics problems 2x faster than those given textbook definitions. The commuter frame forced their brains to simulate traffic jams, not memorize equations.

Danger: Bad metaphors mislead. Calling genes “blueprints” made people assume DNA dictates fate like an architect’s plan—ignoring environment’s role. Precision matters.

Three related subjects are thought as bodily motion, how lies spread faster, and why stories outlast facts.
